title: Compositional Software Design for Agentic Systems
subtitle: A Category-Theoretic Guide to Human-AI Boundaries and Verifiable Engineering
description: A practical guide to designing AI-assisted software systems with composition, diagrams, and effect boundaries that remain auditable and verifiable.
author: ITDO Inc.

chapters.title: Introduction: Why Compositional Design Matters
chapters.description: Purpose, audience, scope, and reading strategy for the book.
objectives: Understand the design problem the book addresses.
Identify the target audience, prerequisites, and expected outcomes.
Choose an effective reading path based on the reader's goals.

chapters.title: Human and AI Responsibility Boundaries
chapters.description: Design the decision rights, artifacts, and control points that keep AI-assisted work accountable.
objectives: Define responsibility boundaries between human reviewers and AI agents.
Identify the minimum artifact set needed for auditability.
Separate safe delegation from judgments that must remain human-led.

chapters.title: Objects, Morphisms, and Composition
chapters.description: Model systems, interfaces, and transformations with the core vocabulary of composition.
objectives: Represent system elements as objects and transformations as morphisms.
Use composition and identity to reason about stable interfaces.
Connect categorical structure to everyday software design decisions.

chapters.title: Diagrams and Commutativity
chapters.description: Use diagrams to test consistency across requirements, architecture, and implementation views.
objectives: Read and create commutative diagrams for design reviews.
Detect broken mappings between multiple system representations.
Turn diagram checks into practical review heuristics.

chapters.title: Functors and Model Translation
chapters.description: Preserve structure while translating between domain, architecture, and runtime models.
objectives: Explain what a structure-preserving translation must keep intact.
Evaluate when a model translation is lossy or misleading.
Apply functorial thinking to layered system design.

chapters.title: Natural Transformations and View Changes
chapters.description: Coordinate alternative views of the same system without breaking consistency.
objectives: Relate multiple functorial views of one design space.
Use naturality to evaluate refactoring and interface adaptation.
Recognize when two views are not coherently aligned.

chapters.title: Universality with Products and Coproducts
chapters.description: Use universal properties to select the simplest correct decomposition or variation point.
objectives: Apply products to combined requirements and shared constraints.
Apply coproducts to alternative behaviors and variability.
Use universal properties as design selection criteria.

chapters.title: Pullbacks and Pushouts for Integration and Migration
chapters.description: Structure integrations and migrations around shared boundaries, provenance, and conflict handling.
objectives: Use pullbacks to reason about constrained joins across systems.
Use pushouts to plan mergers, replacements, and migration paths.
Capture compatibility assumptions before implementation begins.

chapters.title: Monoidal Categories and String Diagrams
chapters.description: Design concurrent workflows, toolchains, and review loops with sequential and parallel composition.
objectives: Distinguish sequential and parallel composition in system workflows.
Read string diagrams as coordination models for people and tools.
Improve failure isolation and handoff design in composed pipelines.

chapters.title: Monads, Kleisli Composition, and Effect Boundaries
chapters.description: Make effects explicit so agent orchestration remains safe, reviewable, and testable.
objectives: Explain why side effects require explicit boundaries.
Use monadic structure to reason about effectful steps.
Apply Kleisli composition to agent and tool orchestration without hiding risk.

chapters.title: Case Study: From Specification to AI-Assisted Implementation
chapters.description: Walk through a full design and delivery flow from problem framing to verified AI-assisted implementation.
objectives: Trace artifacts from specification through implementation and verification.
See how compositional abstractions guide practical design decisions.
Evaluate the limits and extension points of the proposed workflow.

appendices.title: Notation and Diagram Conventions
appendices.description: Shared notation, diagram reading rules, and naming conventions used throughout the book.
objectives: Provide a stable reference for notation and symbols.
Clarify how diagrams and identifiers are interpreted across chapters.

appendices.title: Glossary of Category-Theoretic and Engineering Terms
appendices.description: Definitions and cross-references for the canonical terms used in the book.
objectives: Keep terminology stable across theory, software design, and AI workflows.
Offer a fast lookup source for cross-domain readers.

appendices.title: References and Further Study
appendices.description: Annotated references for theory, engineering practice, and AI-assisted system design.
objectives: Point readers to foundational texts and practical follow-up material.
Provide study paths for deeper work after the core chapters.

appendices.title: Transfer Cases Across Domains
appendices.description: Short transfer caselets that map the book's method into adjacent engineering domains.
objectives: Show how the method transfers beyond repository change review.
Keep the main running example canonical while broadening applicability.
